onboard.summary: A 60-second orientation to the Gee Terminal you're actually working in — what the pieces are and how to drive them.

entries.title: Take the guided tour
entries.note: A guided in-Terminal walkthrough, opened right here in the sidebar. Honest heads-up: it's still being reworked to connect to the live product rather than just describe it.

entries.title: Spaces
entries.note: Each space is its own workspace — separate panels, notepad, and focus. Switch spaces to switch projects without losing your place.

entries.title: Bricks & the sidebar
entries.note: Bricks are live mini-apps docked in the sidebar, each opened by its own hotkey — this guide is one of them. Press B for the Bricks browser to see everything you've got.

entries.title: Your Gees
entries.note: Your workers — same engine, different jobs. You brief each one on what it does; add more as the work grows, and they can hand work off to each other.

entries.title: This guide, two ways
entries.note: Learner (you're here) is what to know — onboarding, a guided tour, and what's new. Flip to Cockpit to get set up: download the Terminal, create your first Gee, and work the checklist.

tour.summary: A followable path through the platform, built for your first weeks: create your first Gee, grow a team, organize with Spaces, then turn the work you repeat into Bricks. Read it top to bottom; it stays current as the platform grows.

modules.title: Create your first Gee
modules.blurb: Set up your first worker, then put it to work.

lessons.title: Run the Create Gee wizard
lessons.note: The first thing you'll do. A short wizard walks you through it: give the Gee a job, a personality, and a schedule. A few minutes and you've got a working teammate.

lessons.title: Just ask
lessons.note: Brief it in plain language, like a teammate. It plans the work, picks its own tools, does it, and tells you what it found or changed. There's nothing to learn beyond saying what you want.

lessons.title: It remembers you
lessons.note: Tell it something once — a preference, who you are, how you like things done — and it carries that forward instead of asking again every time.

lessons.title: It can reach your tools
lessons.note: Connect an account once — calendar, email, Google Drive, Microsoft 365, your bank, the web — and your Gee can act there for you, not just talk about it.

modules.title: Grow your team
modules.blurb: Add specialists and let them work together.

lessons.title: One Gee per job
lessons.note: Your first Gee doesn't have to do everything. Spin up another for a specific role — a writer, a researcher, someone to keep the books — same quick setup, a sharper focus. Add them as the work grows.

lessons.title: Hand work off
lessons.note: A Gee can pass a bounded job to another and fold the result back in. You brief one; several can do the work behind it.

lessons.title: Skills
lessons.note: Packaged know-how a Gee pulls in for a task — draft a doc, build a dashboard, set up another Gee. There's a marketplace to browse, and your Gees pick up their own shortcuts over time.

modules.title: Organize with Spaces
modules.blurb: Give every project and Gee its own room.

lessons.title: A Space is a room for one thing
lessons.note: Its own panels, notepad, and focus. A Space keeps a client project from bleeding into your books or your marketing — switch rooms to switch context.

lessons.title: Make a Space per project or per Gee
lessons.note: Spin one up for each effort or each worker, so everything for it lives in one place. Move between them without losing where you left off.

lessons.title: Put work on a schedule
lessons.note: Give a Gee a standing order — a recurring beat, like a Monday-morning summary — or an objective, a longer goal it keeps chipping at. The work happens without you asking each time.

modules.title: Turn repeated work into a Brick
modules.blurb: Make the work you repeat into a tool you keep.

lessons.title: A Brick is a workflow's home
lessons.note: A small app docked in the sidebar with its own saved state, shared by you and your Gees. The Brick isn't the workflow itself — the workflow is the work your Gees do; the Brick is the home where that work lives and shows up. This Guide is one.

lessons.title: Build a workflow into a Brick
lessons.note: When you catch yourself doing the same thing over and over, crystallize it: a surface with the controls and the saved state for that work, so it stops being a one-off and becomes a place you return to.

lessons.title: Use a Brick day to day
lessons.note: Open it by hotkey, drive it from the panel, and your Gees keep its state current in the background. It's an ongoing home, not a single run — and the platform keeps you posted here as it gains new abilities.

examples.label: Home services

jobs.t: Chases the unpaid invoice the day it ages past terms — in your shop's voice, not a robot's.
Turns a missed call into a booked service window before the lead goes cold.
Files the warranty, the photos, and the notes against the right job, so you can find them in a year.

examples.objective: Chase every invoice the day it ages past terms, and each Monday show me the jobs that are done but still unbilled.

brick.workflow: Every job runs the same loop — missed call, booked window, invoice, then the follow-up when it ages.
brick.name: Dispatch Board
brick.does: Today's calls, open jobs, and unpaid invoices in one panel — you and Dispatch on the same board.

examples.label: Field services

jobs.t: Confirms tomorrow's stops the night before, so the crew rolls without you texting everyone.
Re-books the rained-out visit and tells the customer — you never touch the calendar.
Flags the client who hasn't renewed for the season while there's still time to ask.

examples.objective: Confirm tomorrow's stops every evening, re-book anything rained out, and flag clients who haven't renewed for the season.

brick.workflow: Every evening, the same pass — confirm tomorrow's stops, reshuffle the rain-outs, flag who's due to renew.
brick.name: Route Board
brick.does: Tomorrow's route, the weather holds, and renewals coming up — one panel Routes keeps current overnight.

examples.label: Auto repair
examples.tier: auto repair, tire and service shops

jobs.t: Texts the customer the moment the car's ready, in your shop's voice.
Chases the estimate that's been sitting unanswered, so the bay isn't tied up waiting.
Tells the regulars when service is due — before they drift back to the dealer.

examples.objective: Text customers the moment their car is ready, chase any estimate sitting unanswered, and keep the due-for-service list warm.

brick.workflow: Every car runs the same arc — ready, text the customer, chase the approval, remind them when service is due.
brick.name: Bay Board
brick.does: Every car in the shop with its status, the approvals still sitting, and who's due back in.

examples.label: Accounting firm

jobs.t: Chases the missing client docs before month-end, not at 9pm on the 28th.
Drafts each client's monthly email straight off the numbers, in your firm's tone.
Flags the client whose books look off this month, so a human looks before it ships.

examples.objective: Run the pre-close checklist on every client in parallel, and chase missing docs before month-end — not at 9pm on the 28th.

brick.workflow: Every month-end is the same checklist, run across every client at once.
brick.name: Close Tracker
brick.does: Each client's close in one grid — docs in, email drafted, reviewed, sent.

examples.label: Med spa

jobs.t: Confirms appointments and fills a cancellation off the waitlist before the chair goes empty.
Sends the right aftercare and the rebook reminder on schedule, per treatment.
Follows up on the consult that didn't book the same day, while interest is still warm.

examples.objective: Confirm appointments, fill cancellations off the waitlist, and follow up on every consult that didn't book the same day.

brick.workflow: Every day, the same rhythm — confirm the chairs, fill cancellations, send aftercare, chase the consults.
brick.name: Front Desk Board
brick.does: Today's chairs, the waitlist, aftercare due, and consults still open — one shared panel.

examples.label: Real estate team

jobs.t: Follows up with every new lead inside the hour, in your voice — even mid-showing.
Keeps each listing moving: photos, disclosures, signatures, deadlines.
Nudges the one-year-ago buyer with something useful, so referrals keep coming.

examples.objective: Follow up with every new lead inside the hour in my voice, and keep each active listing's photos, disclosures, and deadlines moving.

brick.workflow: Every lead gets the same first hour; every listing marches the same photos, disclosures, signatures, deadlines.
brick.name: Deal Board
brick.does: Each lead's follow-up clock and each listing's next deadline, in one place you both watch.

changelog.summary: What each gee-code version changes, surfaced on every bump.
changelog.empty_state: No version notes yet — an entry appears here when the platform ships a real, verified change.

download.title: Download the Gee Terminal
download.summary: The Terminal is where Gee lives — your Gees, this guide, and every surface in one place. Download it, open the .dmg, drag Gee to Applications, and launch. Once you're in, this guide keeps updating live, right here.
download.note: Dev channel — the build the Terminal auto-updates from

setup.summary: Create your first Gee here, then bring it alive: connect the tool it works in, give it its standing job, set the one approval gate, and put it on a schedule. Every dial stays tunable in its settings.

steps.title: Create your first Gee
steps.note: Your first worker. Give it a name and a job — it's created right here, with safe defaults you can change later.

provision.role_placeholder: What it does — e.g. research the web and summarize what it finds
provision.hint: Creates a real, working Gee with safe defaults: a sandboxed shell and owner-only email. Manual to start — give it a schedule whenever you like.

steps.title: Connect the tool it works in
steps.note: Your Gee acts inside the tools you already use. Connect the one it needs first — email, calendar, Drive — so it can do the work, not just talk about it. Bring your model(s) here too.

steps.title: Give it its standing job
steps.note: Tell it in plain words the job you want it holding — like briefing a sharp new hire. That standing brief is what it works between the times you talk.

steps.title: Set the one approval gate
steps.note: Decide what it does on its own and what waits for your yes. Any send, spend, or change can hold for approval — set the line and you can turn it loose without worrying.

steps.title: Put it on a schedule
steps.note: Give it a recurring beat — a morning check, a Monday summary — so it moves the work without you asking each time.

steps.title: Fine-tune it anytime
steps.note: Everything you set at creation is a dial you can change later: how much it runs on its own, what needs your approval, which model it uses, its budget, and its schedule. They all live in the Gee's settings.

guides.title: Guides & manuals
guides.summary: The reference shelf. The full manual lives outside the cockpit — everything in here either links out or does something real in the Terminal.

links.label: Gee Guide manual
links.note: The full guide — what Gee is, the tour curriculum, and what's new.

links.label: Interactive tour
links.note: Seven stops — opens as a real panel right here in the Terminal.

links.label: Official Gee operating manual
links.note: The platform's own reference — providers, models, channels, autonomy.

questionnaires.summary: The intake instruments Gee Guide runs onboarding against. Absorb any questionnaire by pasting its doc; Gee structures it here and maps each answer to what it should drive in an onboarding recommendation.
questionnaires.interview_intro: Load what you know about the business — an overview, call notes, a first-meeting doc, a filled-in intake — and Gee recommends the workers to set up and how their work runs as a Gee-Force.
questionnaires.recommendations_empty: No recommendation yet. Load an intake document above (or fill the form) and Gee maps it — reading only what's really there, never inventing — into an expert recommendation: the first workflow to install, the pivot wedge to keep in pocket, the Gee-Force to stand up, the human gates, and the real demo sandbox to launch on a representative task.
